# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, task-scale statistics, evaluation metric definitions, comparative performance results for coding agents on SWE-EVO versus SWE-bench Verified, trajectory failure-mode analysis, and stated benchmark limitations.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: claim-support
citation: SWE-EVO abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-EVO contains 48 software-evolution tasks built from release notes of seven mature open-source Python projects.

## Source Excerpt

The abstract states that SWE-EVO is "Constructed from release notes of seven mature open-source Python projects" and "comprises 48 tasks."

## Note

The source describes this as a Python-only benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: SWE-EVO abstract; Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A SWE-EVO task spans an average of 21 files and is validated against test suites averaging 874 tests per instance.

## Source Excerpt

The abstract says tasks require "multi-step modifications spanning an average of 21 files, validated against test suites averaging 874 tests per instance." Table 1 reports "# Files edited 20.9" and "# Total 874.0."

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The mean SWE-EVO problem statement length is 2,390.5 words, with a maximum of 22,344 words.

## Source Excerpt

Table 1 lists "Issue Text Length (Words) 2390.5" as the mean and "22344" as the max.

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The mean non-test codebase size in SWE-EVO is 363 files and 78K lines, with maxima of 1,046 files and 272K lines.

## Source Excerpt

Table 1 reports "Codebase # Files (non-test) 363 1046" and "# Lines (non-test) 78K 272K."

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The mean gold patch in SWE-EVO edits 610.5 lines, 20.9 files, and 51.0 functions.

## Source Excerpt

Table 1 lists "Gold Patch # Lines edited 610.5," "# Files edited 20.9," and "# Func. edited 51.0."

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Section 3.1 Benchmark Construction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-EVO keeps only candidates whose base commit exactly matches a repository version tag and whose tests show at least one FAIL_TO_PASS transition after the remaining patch is applied.

## Source Excerpt

The paper says candidate instances are selected only when "the base commit corresponds exactly to a version tag of the repository" and then retained only if they "exhibit at least one FAIL_TO_PASS test (i.e., a test that fails pre-patch and passes post-patch)."

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Section 3.2 Evaluation Metrics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Resolved Rate counts an instance as solved only if all FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "The Resolved Rate of an instance is equal 1 if all tests in both FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS are PASSED; otherwise, it is 0."

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Section 3.2 Fix Rate
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Fix Rate credits the fraction of FAIL_TO_PASS tests fixed, but assigns a score of 0 if the model breaks any PASS_TO_PASS test.

## Source Excerpt

The paper states: "The Fix Rate measures progress by computing what fraction of FAIL_TO_PASS tests the model successfully fixes" and "if the model’s patch breaks any PASS_TO_PASS test ... the entire instance receives a score of 0."

## Note

This metric gives partial credit for repaired failing tests but still fail-closes the instance when regressions are introduced.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Abstract; Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
With PR/issue context, GPT-5 resolves 18.75% of SWE-EVO instances with OpenHands and 20.83% with SWE-agent, versus 65.00% on SWE-bench Verified.

## Source Excerpt

The abstract says "GPT-5 with OpenHands achieves only 21% on SWE-EVO versus 65% on SWE-Bench Verified." Table 2 reports "gpt-5-08-07 ... 18.75" for OpenHands, "20.83" for SWE-agent, and "65.00" on SWE-bench Verified.

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Table 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In the release-note-only setting, GPT-5 resolves 14.58% of SWE-EVO with OpenHands and 16.67% with SWE-agent.

## Source Excerpt

Table 3 reports for "gpt-5-08-07 (medium reasoning)" a resolved rate of "14.58" under OpenHands and "16.67" under SWE-agent in the "release-note only setting."

## Note

This is lower than the PR/issue-context setting, indicating some dependence on added context.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: result
citation: Section 4.2; Tables 2 and 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Adding PR/issue context improves SWE-EVO results only modestly.

## Source Excerpt

The paper states: "providing PR/issue context yields measurable but modest gains." For GPT-5, Table 2 versus Table 3 shows OpenHands rising from 14.58% to 18.75% and SWE-agent from 16.67% to 20.83%.

## Note

The source frames the gain as limited rather than transformative, even when extra linked context is provided.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: result
citation: Table 4; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Fix Rate separates models that tie on Resolved Rate; under OpenHands, gpt-4.1 and gpt-oss-120b both resolve 2.08% of instances, but their Fix Rates are 4.65% and 2.08% respectively.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "under the OpenHands scaffold both gpt-4.1 and gpt-oss-120b resolve only 2.08% ... but their Fix Rates are corresponding around 4.65% and 2.08%." Table 4 reports those same values.

## Note

None.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: incident
citation: Section 4.3.1 Trajectory Failure Modes Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Among unresolved GPT-5 SWE-EVO trajectories, instruction-following mistakes account for over 60% of failures, while syntax and tool-use failures are rare.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "Among the GPT-series, the strongest model (gpt-5) almost never fails due to syntax or tool errors; instead, its unresolved cases are dominated by Instruction Following mistakes (over 60%), with a smaller share of Incorrect Implementation."

## Note

This is based on an automated "LLM-as-a-judge" analysis of unresolved SWE-agent trajectories rather than direct test outcomes.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: incident
citation: Limitations
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The authors state that SWE-EVO currently covers only Python projects, relies on release notes as specifications, and has only 48 instances.

## Source Excerpt

In "Limitations," the paper says: "SWE-EVO currently covers only Python projects and relies on release notes as specifications" and "The 48-instance scale, while ensuring quality, limits statistical power for fine-grained comparisons."

## Note

These are explicit scope limits stated by the authors and should remain attached to benchmark-use claims.
---

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-004
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Section 3.1 Benchmark Construction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.18470v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-EVO keeps only candidates whose base commit exactly matches a repository version tag and whose tests show at least one FAIL_TO_PASS transition after the remaining patch is applied.

## Source Excerpt

The paper says candidate instances are selected only when "the base commit corresponds exactly to a version tag of the repository" and then retained only if they "exhibit at least one FAIL_TO_PASS test (i.e., a test that fails pre-patch and passes post-patch)."

## Note

None.
```

contradiction_relevance: none
---
Resolved Rate counts an instance as solved only if all FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "The Resolved Rate of an instance is equal 1 if all tests in both FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS are PASSED; otherwise, it is 0."

## Note

None.
```
